response:
Short answer
- fazure-mcp is fronted by an Azure Application Gateway + WAF and uses OAuth for normal client auth, plus network‑level trust (Rackspace VPN / trusted IPs) for internal traffic. WAF custom rules (VpnOnly, RateLimit, BlockKnownPath, etc.) and per‑listener/site policies control who reaches the OAuth and MCP endpoints.

Details / components and flow
- Ingress: Azure Application Gateway (App Gateway) with WAF protecting fazure-mcp.mscloud.rackspace.com.
- Primary authentication:
  - OAuth (fazure-oauth) for the registration/auth flows (e.g. POST /register).
  - Token-based/trusted network access for internal systems: Rackspace VPN IPs and Janus AWS IPs are explicitly allowlisted.
- WAF policy model:
  - There is a global WAF policy with AllowTrustedVpn rules and OWASP CRS managed rules.
  - Some listeners/sites use per‑listener/site WAF policies which were created by copying managed rules and custom rules (RateLimit, VpnOnly, BlockSensitiveFiles, BlockKnownPath, GeoBlocking, AllowSafeFilters). If the global AllowTrustedVpn rule is not copied into a per‑site policy, trusted VPN traffic can be blocked (caused the 403s observed).
  - Managed OWASP inspection can produce SQLi/other false positives on internal MCP traffic; a mitigation pattern was implemented to exempt trusted internal MCP SQL traffic from those managed inspections.
- Recent operational changes:
  - Added full Janus AWS address set to trusted IP allowlists for fazure MCP and oauth endpoints on App Gateway to stop legitimate traffic being blocked.
  - Adjusted site/global WAF custom rule priorities to avoid blocking legitimate OAuth redirect_uris and similar flows.

If you want, I can produce a diagram or a precise sequence diagram of the auth flow (external OAuth client → App Gateway/WAF → oauth service → MCP backend; internal trusted client → Rackspace VPN/allowlist → App Gateway bypass/mitigation → MCP backend).

Root cause was that recent POST /mcp failures on fazure-mcp.mscloud.rackspace.com at 2026-04-14T17:51:41Z through 2026-04-14T18:06:13Z were blocked by custom rule VpnOnly under policy scope fazure-mcp, even though those IPs are the known JanusAWS set already present in the global policy. Fixed live by updating site policy waf-appgw-phoenix-prod-southcent", "2026-04-15: On 2026-04-15, investigation of fazure-mcp 500/502/403 traffic found that the current issue was not WAF regression and not backend 500s. In AGWAccessLogs for fazure-mcp.mscloud.rackspace.com over the prior 24h, there were 0 HTTP 500 responses, multiple 502s concentrated around 13:46-13:50 UTC and 14:14-14:15 UTC and especially 14:38-14:51 UTC, and a small number of 403s. AGWFirewallLogs showed trusted Rackspace/Janus traffic matching AllowTrustedVpnFazureMcp and current 403s were mainly expected VpnOnly blocks from external/scanner or preview IPs such as 146.70.189.237 and 52.123.190.89/125. T", "2026-03-25: On 2026-03-25, investigated fazure-mcp WAF 403s for POST /mcp from Rackspace VPN IP 72.32.180.181. Local Azure-MCP rollout artifacts showed the per-listener WAF policy waf-appgw-phoenix-prod-southcentralus-rax-fazure-mcp-sites was created by copying managedRules and policySettings from the global policy, then attaching only site custom rules RateLimit, VpnOnly, BlockSensitiveFiles, BlockKnownPath, GeoBlocking, and AllowSafeFilters. The global RackspaceVPN Allow rule was not copied into the site policy. Result: trusted VPN traffic still traverses OWASP CRS 3.2 managed rules on /mcp and legitima", "2026-03-25: On 2026-03-25, a reusable WAF mitigation pattern was established for trusted internal MCP SQL traffic behind Azure Application Gateway. Rackspace VPN IP 72.32.180.181 was receiving 403s on POST /mcp to fazure-mcp.mscloud.rackspace.com because the per-listener WAF policy waf-appgw-phoenix-prod-southcentralus-rax-fazure-mcp-sites allowed the VPN through the VpnOnly rule but still sent the JSON request body through OWASP managed inspection. AGWFirewallLogs showed SQL injection false positives on ARGS:params.arguments.query with rules 920230, 942100, 942150, 942260, 942360, 942370, 942380, 942410,", "2026-03-25: On 2026-03-25, investigated 403s from Rackspace VPN IP 72.32.180.178 to fazure-oauth.mscloud.rackspace.com/register. Live AGW logs showed OPTIONS /register returned 200, while POST /register was blocked by global WAF policy waf-appgw-phoenix-prod-southcentralus-rax with rule 931130 (Possible Remote File Inclusion / Off-Domain Reference/Link) on client_uri=https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/inspector and redirect_uris=http://localhost:6274/oauth/callback/debug, followed by 949110 anomaly score block. Updated global WAF custom rules by moving BlockKnownPath from priority 5 to 6 and adding A", "2026-03-19: On 2026-03-19 in /Users/benj8956/Downloads/Azure-MCP, updated scripts/fazure_lockdown.sh and applied the expanded App Gateway/WAF lockdown for appgw-phoenix-prod-southcentralus-rax. The shared RateLimit rule at priority 1 and FazureMcpVpnOnly rule at priority 7 now cover four MCP hosts: fazure-mcp.mscloud.rackspace.com, fazure-mcp-dev.mscloud.rackspace.com, fazure-cosmos-mcp.mscloud.rackspace.com, and fazure-cosmos-mcp-dev.mscloud.rackspace.com. OAuth remains scoped separately under FazureOauthVpnOnly. Added backend access-restriction backup/apply/rollback/validation coverage for azure-mcp-dev", "2026-03-17: Resolved the MFA Phase 2 ARM template deployment issue. The problem was that the ARM template specified resource scopes that didn't align with the new MFA policy requirements. When MFA was enforced, the service principal couldn't authenticate because the ARM scope was set to subscription-level but the MFA policy required resource-group-level scope. Fix: Updated ARM scope definitions to use resource-group-level scoping. Used az rest instead of curl for the API calls because az rest handles token refresh and MFA challenges automatically, while curl required manual token management."]}
